full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Once again, the people of God gather at this font just in front of me in this table, just<|7.76|> <|7.76|> behind me in the wake of another mass shooting.<|12.68|> <|12.68|> And I wrote this yesterday evening, and there's been one since then.<|17.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One at a garlic festival in Gilroy, California,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> one at a Walmart in El Paso, Texas,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> and one at a nightclub in Dayton, Ohio.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> Just as in other instances, the people who were murdered by the gunmen<|19.00|> <|19.00|> did not wake up that morning or eat lunch that afternoon<|24.00|> <|24.00|> knowing that that day would be their last.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> No person expects to take her children shopping at the closest Walmart<|6.50|> <|6.50|> in preparation for the first day of school,<|8.94|> <|8.94|> only to end up in a morgue later that day.<|12.88|> <|12.88|> That mother doesn't wake up thinking that because that,<|16.12|> <|16.12|> she doesn't wake up thinking that because she should not have to.<|21.12|> <|22.68|> No one should wake up with that on their mind.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And yet, there are forces at work in this society that disproportionately benefit from<|8.72|> <|8.72|> the buying and selling and lobbying of arms, of selling, of stifling the imagination of<|19.86|> <|19.86|> a culture.<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> of bringing us to our knees not to revere God,<|6.06|> <|6.82|> but to revere the instruments of war.<|10.52|> <|12.64|> Some time ago, I read Audre Lorde's essay,<|17.50|> <|17.50|> A Burst of Light,<|19.76|> <|20.60|> a series of journal entries she wrote<|22.96|> <|22.96|> after being diagnosed with cancer a second time.<|27.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> On November 17, 1986, she reflected in the following way,<|7.20|> <|7.20|> "How has everyday living changed for me with the advent of a second cancer?<|15.00|> <|15.00|> I moved through a terrible and invigorating savor of now,<|22.00|> <|22.00|> a visceral awareness of the passage of time with its nightmare and its energy.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> No more long-term loans, extended payments, 20-year plans,<|7.74|> <|7.74|> pay my debts, call the tickets in, the charges, the emotional IOUs.<|13.28|> <|13.28|> Now is the time, if ever, once and for all,<|17.12|> <|17.12|> to alter the patterns of isolation.<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "Remember that nice lady down the street," she continues,<|4.66|> <|4.66|> "whose sun you used to cross at the light and who was always saying,<|8.46|> <|8.46|> 'Now, if there's ever anything I can do for you, just let me know.'"<|12.76|> <|12.76|> "Well, her boy's got strong muscles and the lawn needs mowing."<|18.16|> <|18.16|> End quote.<|20.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Audre Lorde's reflection is a model of attentiveness and single-mindedness in the face of profound<|11.34|> <|11.34|> bewilderment and uncertainty.<|16.24|> <|16.24|> Remember that nice lady down the street who has a physically strong son and who offers<|20.58|> <|20.58|> to do anything for me?<|22.24|> <|22.24|> Well, my lawn needs mowing.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and a second cancer diagnosis will bring that into focus.<|5.34|> <|7.00|> Here, this author attends to the now, the present.<|14.12|> <|15.98|> She pays her debts and does away with 20-year plans,<|19.32|> <|19.32|> and with prophetic precision, she takes stock of her life<|24.06|> <|24.06|> and governs herself accordingly.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> with great wisdom.<|2.22|> <|4.58|> Communities and societies and churches and families and nations<|10.68|> <|10.68|> are capable of such wisdom.<|13.74|> <|14.86|> We are capable of such wisdom,<|18.36|> <|18.36|> even though at times it may not seem that way.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we like Audre Lorde are able to muster the inner focus it takes<|5.98|> <|5.98|> to engender a future and a present<|10.18|> <|10.18|> in which human lives are valued over profits.<|15.28|> <|16.68|> Valued over profits and the impulse to tear down large arms factories<|22.70|> <|22.70|> for even larger ones is resisted.<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But sometimes that sort of deep wisdom comes only when we have reached what appears to be our end.<|8.62|> <|9.58|> That breath just before our final one.<|12.78|> <|13.16|> That second diagnosis.<|14.50|> <|15.06|> When we've heard enough cries from the blood of holy innocence.<|19.06|> <|19.26|> When we see more scenes involving mass murder than mass joy.<|24.28|> <|24.28|> Amen.<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> when we have grown weary from accepting the world as it is<|4.76|> <|4.76|> instead of demanding a world as it is in God's imagination.<|10.64|> <|12.58|> The mother who wakes up only thinking about the menial tasks of the day<|17.58|> <|17.58|> and how many strollers she needs instead of how many bulletproof vests she needs<|22.48|> <|22.48|> is already living in that imagination.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And she, not the gunman, is our model.<|5.28|> <|5.28|> She is our patron. She is our saint.<|10.32|> <|10.32|> Violence toward vulnerable populations is almost always a failure of imagination.<|19.76|> <|19.76|> A failure of imagination.<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> An ability to see things as they could be.<|5.12|> <|6.30|> It is waking up and saying, and this is actually the perfect reading for this.<|13.44|> <|14.80|> So when we have a failure of imagination, we wake up and say things like...<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> perfectly pointless.<|3.80|> <|3.80|> I realize that everything is pointless,<|8.60|> <|8.60|> is what we just heard from Ecclesiastes.<|11.60|> <|11.60|> And we have the power, unfortunately, in that pointlessness,<|18.80|> <|18.80|> to impose despair in violent ways over other people.<|25.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> which is why nihilists and pyromaniacs should not be trusted with the common good.<|11.28|> <|13.42|> Dreamers, poets, musicians, prophets, comedians,<|23.46|> <|23.46|> they should be trusted with the common good.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> which is why when the banning of assault weapons is spoken of,<|3.96|> <|3.96|> I cannot help but think of the prophet Isaiah's vision,<|8.94|> <|9.46|> in which is said this,<|12.04|> <|13.02|> quote,<|13.48|> <|15.14|> Then they will beat their swords into iron plows<|20.42|> <|20.42|> and their spears into pruning tools.<|23.62|> <|23.62|> Quote,<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Nation will not take up sword against nation.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> Nation will not take up assault weapon against nation.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> Nation will not take up bomb against nation.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> Nation will not take up gun against nation.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> They will no longer learn how to make war.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.72|> I descend from people who wrote the Negro spiritual.<|6.50|> <|6.50|> I'm going to lay down my burden down by the riverside<|11.92|> <|12.96|> and study war no more.<|16.32|> <|16.32|> Ain't going to study war no more.<|20.64|> <|20.64|> That spiritual was inspired by this vision in Isaiah.<|25.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Instruments of war are meant to be farming implements.<|6.62|> <|8.26|> Instruments of cultivation and growth and creativity and nourishment<|14.60|> <|14.60|> that leaves us with commas.<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.52|> and semicolons,<|3.78|> <|5.46|> not the periods of imagination failure.<|10.24|> <|12.08|> And so, may we, with God's help,<|16.88|> <|16.88|> take these weapons of violence and fashion them into something different.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.32|> Something imaginative.<|3.96|> <|4.96|> Something wise.<|8.60|>
